summary: Jeff Atwood revisits his 2007 article about programmers who can't program, sharing new evidence that the problem persists. He highlights an email from Mike Lin describing how he streamlined technical interviews to quickly identify candidates who cannot write even basic code. The post advocates for immediate technical screening, preferably via phone or web-based coding tools, to filter out non-programmers before wasting interview time. Atwood expresses frustration that people with no programming ability continue applying for programming jobs—and apparently sometimes getting hired. He concludes that inadequate industry-wide interviewing standards are degrading the profession.
key_points: The majority of programming job applicants cannot write even simple programs, a problem that persisted from 2007 to 2010
Leading with technical questions rather than rapport-building dramatically reduces time wasted on unqualified candidates
Phone and web-based coding interviews can identify non-programmers in 5-15 minutes
Inadequate interviewing standards across the industry allow non-programmers to get hired, which degrades the profession
Simple technical screening should be mandatory for every programming interview
money_quotes: Many candidates who show up for programming job interviews can't program. At all.
It's the equivalent of attempting to hire a truck driver and finding out that 90 percent of the job applicants can't find the gas pedal or the gear shift.
I schedule an actual interview with them if they can at least write a simple 10-line program. That doesn't happen often.
Why do people who can't write a simple program even entertain the idea they can get jobs as working programmers?
At least bad programmers can be educated; non-programming programmers are not only hopeless but also cheapen the careers of everyone around them.
Our industry-wide interviewing standards for programmers are woefully inadequate, and that's a disgrace.
key_insight: Simple technical screening at the start of every programming interview is essential because an alarming number of applicants for programming jobs cannot program at all.
